---
title: "Limitations"
---

This page is the honest account.
A privacy tool that oversells itself is worse than none, because it replaces caution with confidence.

::: {.callout-warning}

## Read the output before you send it

Every limitation below has the same mitigation.
The sanitised file is a few seconds' reading, and that reading is part of the workflow rather than an optional extra.

:::

## The model cannot tell a name from a phrase

This is the sharpest limitation, and it is not something a better setting fixes.

Measured on the shipped model:

| Text | Score |
| --- | --- |
| `Thomas Bernard`, inside a document | 0.237 |
| `The quick brown fox` | 0.218 |

They are eighteen thousandths apart.
No threshold separates a real name from an ordinary phrase, so the tool redacts both and expects you to put back what it got wrong.

That is why [`oboro review`](reference.qmd#review) exists.

Two consequences follow.
Ordinary prose sometimes comes back with holes in it, which is a nuisance.
And a name may still be missed, which is not.

## Context changes what the model sees

The same name scores 0.47 alone and 0.24 once surrounded by a document.

The default threshold is calibrated on whole documents for exactly this reason.
An earlier version was tuned on single sentences and silently missed a name in a real file, which is the failure this tool exists to prevent.

## Identifiers that fail their own checksum are not found

A mistyped IBAN, a SIREN with a digit dropped, a card number that fails Luhn: none are detected.

The check is what makes the rules trustworthy rather than noisy, so it stays.
Add a [custom pattern](configuration.qmd#patterns) if your documents carry identifiers that do not validate.

## Scanned PDFs are refused, not read

A PDF whose pages are images yields almost no text.
Rather than hand back a nearly empty file that looks sanitised, `oboro` refuses it and says why.

Reading those pages needs rasterising them, which needs a large external library the tool does not carry.
Export the pages as images and pass those to a build with `--features ocr`.

## Formats it does not read

Older `.doc`, `.xls` and `.ppt`, along with `.pptx`, are not read at all.
Neither is anything embedded inside a document: a spreadsheet pasted into a Word file is not reached.

## Recognition accuracy on real images is not tested

The OCR path compiles and its failure paths are covered, but no automated test feeds Tesseract a photograph and checks the result.
Treat text recovered from images as less reliable than text read directly.

## What redaction does not hide

Replacing a name with `[[PERSON_1]]` removes the name.
It does not remove the shape of the story around it.

A document describing a dispute with a named supplier in a named town in a named month may identify everyone involved even with every name replaced.
Structured identifiers are the part this tool can handle; inference from context is not, and no placeholder scheme solves it.

## Language coverage

The rules are written for French and English documents.
The address patterns in particular are French.
The recognition model is multilingual, but the tool has been exercised mostly on those two languages.
